era_context: Shalmaneser III's reign continues in Assyria; the empire is in an expansionist phase. In the Levant, city-states and kingdoms are responding to Assyrian pressure, beginning to form defensive coalitions. Greece remains in the Archaic period, with city-states developing independently. Egypt's 22nd Dynasty continues under Osorkon II (r. 872–837 BCE). China's Spring and Autumn period sees continued fragmentation of the Zhou realm.
geographic_coverage_gaps: South Asia (limited written records for this specific year)

events.title: Continuation of Shalmaneser III's Levantine Campaigns
events.description: Shalmaneser III continues military operations in the Levant, targeting Aramean states and other regional powers. These campaigns are part of a sustained effort to establish Assyrian hegemony. The specific events of 863 BCE are not individually distinguished in surviving sources; this year falls within the broader campaign period documented in Shalmaneser's annals. The campaigns generate tribute and vassalage from conquered or intimidated states.

sources.name: Shalmaneser III's Annals (reconstructed from multiple inscriptions)

events.certainty_note: Shalmaneser III's campaigns are well-attested, but the specific year 863 BCE is assigned by chronological convention based on regnal year counts. Individual years within his reign are not always distinctly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events of 863 BCE are prominently attested in surviving sources. The year may be historically significant only as part of a longer campaign sequence, not as a distinct turning point. Modern scholarship does not isolate 863 BCE as a year of particular importance in Assyrian or Levantine history.
historiographic_note: For 863 BCE, the historical record is even thinner than for 864 BCE. Assyrian annals group campaigns by regnal year, but individual years are often not separately narrated unless a major event (battle, siege, treaty) occurred. The absence of specific documentation for 863 BCE may reflect either a year of routine military operations or simply the loss of detailed records. The bias toward Assyrian sources remains dominant.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of the campaign sequence begun in 864 BCE
